author: @amievil3697
text: The cracker barrel's history as a food-storage item is tied to the rise of mass-produced soda crackers in the 19th century, which were shipped in large wooden barrels to general stores for preservation. After their contents were consumed, these barrels were repurposed as communal gathering spots, often with checkerboards on top, serving as informal community hubs for discussion and socializing.

author: @Ka_Gg
text:
I went to business school probably around the time of these ladies. I can see it at that time that everything was based upon appearance and make YOUR brand. It wasn't actually about being competent and being good at things, it was about finding ways to stick out. Also, they started hinting at be careful avoid you put online because it might haunt you down the road. What's a really ingrained is that when you are in charge, punish people who don't say what you want online. I could see all this stuff happening before it manifested. 

There was also a push to do things outside of work. Don't worry about being great at your job, just go out in the community and try to look good. Some of the most incompetent clowns in my classes were the first ones to get good jobs. The ones of us that were smart and really focused on learning would laugh about it. 

All I can say is that you need to keep your eyes open and watch things as like progresses. If so, you often see things happen well before everybody else does

author: @KSigKid
text:
Jeremy talks about McDonald's all the time, and he's right that it's absolutely pathetic to walk into now. They are unrecognizable inside.

But this started so long ago, and it's similar to why we don't have great cartoons anymore. McD's marketed to kids, and when the outrage started happening about kids being fat and fast food being awful for anyone, they started introducing healthy options and sides while erasing the child lures.

It's more about avoiding lawsuits than anything for them.

author: @jamiebraswell5520
text:
Well, my family and I have been fans of Cracker Barrel ever since the 1980s when I was a young teenager.  My dad worked in Iowa and we stumbled across one close to Ft. Madison.  We went there a lot that summer.  We were thrilled a year or so later when we were getting one in North Alabama where we live.  It was the only one in North Alabama for years before more slowly started popping up.  It was a family favorite.  I have many fond memories of family gatherings for dinner, or sometimes breakfast.  When family from out of state came to visit, we would have large breakfast gatherings at Cracker Barrel.  Great memories.

After my father passed away in 2023, I would take my mother out to Cracker Barrel.  It was familiar to her since she had dementia (she still knew us and was never combative).  Late October 2024 was the last time we went out before she became bed bound, and it was to Cracker Barrel.  She passed away at the end of May this year, 2025.  I am glad that my parents aren't here to see Cracker Barrel get destroyed.  That might sound silly to some, but it was a staple in our family life for 40 years.

As for McDonalds, as an adult I have never liked their food and so I never go there.  Aside from the generic outside rennovation, I have no idea how the interior looks.  I remember loving McDonalds back in the 1970s when I was very young.  Ronald, Grimace, Hamburgler, Mayor McCheese...it was all so awesome.  I had a big toy of the McDonalds restaurant, with a full kitchen, little Fischer Price people, and accessories.  The thing was huge!  It had a mat with a road and parking area. And a spin toy for the outdoors.  McDonalds was something special back then!

I haven't eaten indoors at fast food places in years, so the transformation to generic has not been something I have truly noticed.  I believe you, though!
